From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Road running competition over a distance of ten kilometres

"10 kilometres" redirects here. For the order of magnitude, see [1 myriametre](/wiki/1_myriametre "1 myriametre"). For the track running event, see [10,000 metres](/wiki/10,000_metres "10,000 metres").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7c/2012_Bupa_London_10000_Blackfriars_Bridge.jpg/250px-2012_Bupa_London_10000_Blackfriars_Bridge.jpg)](/wiki/File:2012_Bupa_London_10000_Blackfriars_Bridge.jpg)

The mass public race at the 2012 [London 10000](/wiki/London_10000 "London 10000") race

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b7/PeachtreeRoadRace2006FinishLine.jpg/250px-PeachtreeRoadRace2006FinishLine.jpg)](/wiki/File:PeachtreeRoadRace2006FinishLine.jpg)

Amateur runners completing the very large 2006 [Peachtree Road Race](/wiki/Peachtree_Road_Race "Peachtree Road Race")

The **10K run** is a [long-distance](/wiki/Long-distance_running "Long-distance running") [road running](/wiki/Road_running "Road running") competition over a distance of ten kilometres (6.2 miles). Also referred to as the **10K road race**, **10 km**, or simply **10K**, it is one of the most common types of road running event, alongside the shorter [5K](/wiki/5K_run "5K run") and longer [half marathon](/wiki/Half_marathon "Half marathon") and [marathon](/wiki/Marathon "Marathon"). It is usually distinguished from the [10,000 metres](/wiki/10,000_metres "10,000 metres") [track running](/wiki/Track_running "Track running") event by stating the distance in kilometres, rather than metres.

As one of the shortest common road distances, many 10K races attract high levels of public participation. Among the [largest 10K races](/wiki/List_of_largest_running_events "List of largest running events"), the [Peachtree Road Race](/wiki/Peachtree_Road_Race "Peachtree Road Race") in [Atlanta](/wiki/Atlanta "Atlanta"), United States had over 55,000 participants in 2011, while the [Vancouver Sun Run](/wiki/Vancouver_Sun_Run "Vancouver Sun Run") and [Bolder Boulder](/wiki/Bolder_Boulder "Bolder Boulder") both had close to 50,000 runners.[[1]](#cite_note-1)[[2]](#cite_note-2)[[3]](#cite_note-3) The popularity of 10K races lies in the fact that, for most adults, the 10K distance is long enough to represent a challenge but short enough to remain accessible for an untrained runner.[[4]](#cite_note-4)

Most popular 10K races are an annual fixture in a city or area and typically incorporate an element of [charity running](/wiki/Charity_running "Charity running"), where participants raise funds for a cause, based upon their completion of the course. Public members may participate in the races as a competition or simply for pleasure as a [fun run](/wiki/Fun_run "Fun run"). Some races also allow [wheelchair racers](/wiki/Wheelchair_racing "Wheelchair racing") to enter. Traditional New Year's Day races are often held over 10K, including the [San Silvestre Vallecana](/wiki/San_Silvestre_Vallecana "San Silvestre Vallecana") in Spain.

The accessibility of the distance, and road running in general, has meant that local governments and health charities often form partnerships with races as a way of promoting [physical fitness](/wiki/Physical_fitness "Physical fitness") among the [general public](/wiki/General_public "General public").[[5]](#cite_note-5) Medical organisations, fitness groups, drinks manufacturers and [sportswear](/wiki/Sportswear_(activewear) "Sportswear (activewear)") companies are typical [commercial sponsors](/wiki/Commercial_sponsors "Commercial sponsors") of 10K events.[[6]](#cite_note-6)[[7]](#cite_note-7)

The 10-kilometre [metric](/wiki/Metric_system "Metric system") distance has been used for road running events for a large portion of the modern era of [athletics](/wiki/Athletics_(sport) "Athletics (sport)") – the [Běchovice](/wiki/B%C4%9Bchovice "Běchovice")–[Prague](/wiki/Prague "Prague") race is one of the longest-running events over the distance, having first been held in 1897.[[8]](#cite_note-8)[[9]](#cite_note-9) In Western countries using [imperial measurements](/wiki/Imperial_measurements "Imperial measurements"), the 6-mile run (9.7 km) was once more prevalent, but many long-running events (such as the [Cincinnati Thanksgiving Day Race](/w/index.php?title=Cincinnati_Thanksgiving_Day_Race&action=edit&redlink=1 "Cincinnati Thanksgiving Day Race (page does not exist)") and [Saltwell Road Race](/w/index.php?title=Saltwell_Road_Race&action=edit&redlink=1 "Saltwell Road Race (page does not exist)")) have adapted their courses to match the metric distance.[[10]](#cite_note-10)

## Professional 10K running

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=10K_run&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Professional 10K running")]

At the professional level, many races offer significant [prize money](/wiki/Prize_money "Prize money") to athletes who achieve a high finishing position in the race. At the highest level, annual prize money can total over US$100,000 at races such as the [World's Best 10K](/wiki/World%27s_Best_10K "World's Best 10K"), Peachtree Road Race, Apryle Showers Run - Florida's Fastest 10K, and [World 10K Bangalore](/wiki/World_10K_Bangalore "World 10K Bangalore").[[11]](#cite_note-11)[[12]](#cite_note-12)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fa/2010_BUPA_London_10k.jpg/250px-2010_BUPA_London_10k.jpg)](/wiki/File:2010_BUPA_London_10k.jpg)

[Mo Farah](/wiki/Mo_Farah "Mo Farah"), [Micah Kogo](/wiki/Micah_Kogo "Micah Kogo") and [Chris Thompson](/wiki/Chris_Thompson_(athlete) "Chris Thompson (athlete)") in the elite men's race at the [London 10000](/wiki/London_10000 "London 10000") in 2010

The 10K road distance has never been featured on the event programmes of the [Athletics at the Summer Olympics](/wiki/Athletics_at_the_Summer_Olympics "Athletics at the Summer Olympics") or the [IAAF World Championships in Athletics](/wiki/IAAF_World_Championships_in_Athletics "IAAF World Championships in Athletics"). However, it briefly had its own individual championship for women in the form of the [IAAF World Women's Road Race Championships](/wiki/IAAF_World_Women%27s_Road_Race_Championships "IAAF World Women's Road Race Championships"), contested over ten kilometres in 1983 and 1984.[[13]](#cite_note-13) As a result, the highest level 10K road competitions occur at individual races run by race promoters, who attract elite international level runners through prize money and [appearance fees](/w/index.php?title=Appearance_fee&action=edit&redlink=1 "Appearance fee (page does not exist)"). These races are held in all parts of the world, but the highest calibre races are mainly concentrated in the United States, Canada, Europe and [East Asia](/wiki/East_Asia "East Asia").

The world records for the 10K road distance are 26:24 minutes for men ([Rhonex Kipruto](/wiki/Rhonex_Kipruto "Rhonex Kipruto"), 2020) and 29:43 minutes for women ([Joyciline Jepkosgei](/wiki/Joyciline_Jepkosgei "Joyciline Jepkosgei"), 2017).[[14]](#cite_note-Topmen-14)[[15]](#cite_note-Topwomen-15) Performances over ten kilometres on the roads were not recognised as [world records](/wiki/List_of_world_records_in_athletics "List of world records in athletics") by the [International Association of Athletics Federations](/wiki/International_Association_of_Athletics_Federations "International Association of Athletics Federations") (IAAF) until 2003. Instead, the fastest times were referred to as "world bests". This changed in August 2003 when the IAAF Congress approved world record status for a number of specified road distances, including the 10 km.[[16]](#cite_note-16)

As with other forms of professional [long-distance running](/wiki/Long-distance_running "Long-distance running"), East African athletes have been dominant in the 10K distance since the 1990s. As of January 2020, all ten of the fastest male 10K runners are East African (five of them [Kenyan](/wiki/Kenya "Kenya")), while nine of the top ten female runners are Kenyan.
